elements.sent-2411-001: Continuous-valued Autoregressive (AR) image generation models have demonstrated notable superiority over their discrete-token counterparts, showcasing considerable reconstruction quality and higher generation fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the computational demands of the autoregressive framework result in significant inference overhead.
elements.sent-2411-003: While speculative decoding has proven effective in accelerating Large Language Models (LLMs), their adaptation to continuous-valued visual autoregressive models remains unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-004: This work generalizes the speculative decoding algorithm from discrete tokens to continuous space.
elements.sent-2411-005: By analyzing the intrinsic properties of output distribution, we establish a tailored acceptance criterion for the diffusion distributions prevalent in such models.
elements.sent-2411-006: To overcome the inconsistency that occurred in speculative decoding output distributions, we introduce denoising trajectory alignment and token pre-filling methods.
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, we identify the hard-to-sample distribution in the rejection phase.
elements.sent-2411-008: To mitigate this issue, we propose a meticulous acceptance-rejection sampling method with a proper upper bound, thereby circumventing complex integration.
elements.sent-2411-009: Experimental results show that our continuous speculative decoding achieves a remarkable $2.33\times$ speed-up on off-the-shelf models while maintaining the output distribution.
elements.sent-2411-011: Autoregressive (AR) models have shown significant superiority in image generation tasks~\cite{van2016pixelrnn,van2016pixelcnn,chen2018pixelsnail,esser2021taming,ramesh2021zero,li2023mage,yu2023magvit,chang2023muse,VAR .}
elements.sent-2411-012: They predict next token sequentially based on previously generated tokens.
elements.sent-2411-013: Typically, input images are transformed from pixel space into a discrete token space through vector quantization (VQ), and then AR models predict the next token as a classification task.
elements.sent-2411-014: This approach has demonstrated considerable potential in image generation.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, the VQ operation induces instability during training and may be insufficient for capturing the nuanced image details~\cite{yu2023magvit,mentzer2023finite .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Recently, an alternative approach utilizes diffusion models~\cite{ho2020ddpm,nichol202ddim to embed visual tokens into a continuous distribution~\cite{tschannen2025givt,li2024mar,xu2024discodiff .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The next token prediction is performed through a denoising process conditioned by the outputs of the autoregressive model.
elements.sent-2411-018: They not only mitigate the issues associated with discrete vector quantization but also facilitate the generation of higher-quality images~\cite{gu2024dart,fan2024fluid .}

Discrete models can conveniently compute output probabilities and be sampled from modified distributions. In contrast, continuous models require determining how to compute probabilities, and sampling from modified distributions via draft and target output distributions is often more challenging. } \label{fig:figure_intro}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, the inference of the autoregressive model is slow and expensive due to sequential decoding.
elements.sent-2411-020: Speculative decoding~\cite{leviathan2023fast,chen2023acc in LLMs aims to reduce the inference cost via draft \& verification.}
elements.sent-2411-021: It involves a smaller draft model generating a sequence of draft tokens $p(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-022: Then, a more accurate target model $q(x)$ would verify each token to decide whether to accept or reject it and then resample a new one.
elements.sent-2411-023: Nonetheless, speculative decoding has not been adapted to continuous-valued autoregressive models.
elements.sent-2411-024: Recent works ~\cite{teng2024sjd,jang2024lantern are introduced to the discrete tokens, but the application to the continuous probability density function (PDF) is not feasible.}
elements.sent-2411-025: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:figure_intro , it introduces the following challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Furthermore, distinct inconsistency exists between the draft and target outputs, leading to a low acceptance rate during speculative decoding.
elements.sent-2411-027: However, this distribution involves complex integration, which makes it challenging to derive an analytical form and to sample directly.
elements.sent-2411-028: To this end, we introduce Continuous Speculative Decoding, the first to extend the speculative decoding algorithm to the continuous token space.
elements.sent-2411-029: To establish the acceptance criterion $p(x)/q(x)$, we derive an appropriate calculation method to obtain the two PDFs through a comprehensive analysis of the diffusion process at the output end.
elements.sent-2411-030: To enhance output consistency, we introduce a denoising trajectory alignment to align the output distributions of the draft and target models.
elements.sent-2411-031: Also, for the inconsistency in the autoregressive process, we introduce a token pre-filling strategy to mitigate the issue of low early acceptance rates and enhance the overall acceptance rate without compromising inference speed.
elements.sent-2411-032: For the rejection and resampling phase, we employ acceptance-rejection sampling~\cite{casella2004generalized to the modified PDF, which lacks an analytical form.}
elements.sent-2411-033: By setting proper upper bounds, we successfully avoid the complex integration and simplify the sampling of the modified distribution.
elements.sent-2411-034: Similar to traditional speculative decoding, under these configurations, our method ensures that the output distribution of the speculative decoding process remains consistent with that of the target model alone.
elements.sent-2411-035: Our continuous speculative decoding framework can be integrated seamlessly with existing continuous-valued visual autoregressive models without additional training procedures, alterations to the model architectures, or changes to the output distribution.
elements.sent-2411-036: Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithm via qualitative and quantitative evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-037: Furthermore, we measure and compare the wall-time improvements on open-sourced MAR~\cite{li2024mar models with various configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-038: We also report the performance metrics using Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)~\cite{heusel2017fid and Inception Score (IS)~\cite{salimans2016is on ImageNet $256\times 256$ generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:teaser , our continuous speculative decoding algorithm achieves an outstanding inference speedup by up to $2.33\times$ while maintaining the original generation quality to a large extent.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Our contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-041: We are the first to extend speculative decoding to continuous space and adapt it for continuous-valued autoregressive image generation.
elements.sent-2411-042: We establish an appropriate acceptance criterion for continuous PDF and an integral-free sampling method through acceptance-rejection sampling of the modified distribution, which lacks an analytical form.
elements.sent-2411-043: We derive denoising trajectory alignment to align the distributions and a token pre-filling method to address the issue of low initial step acceptance rates.
elements.sent-2411-044: Without additional training, modifications on model architecture, or changes of output distribution, our method achieves $2.33\times$ speedup and comparable image quality.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=2.0\columnwidth]{figures/main.pdf} \caption{ The overview of our proposed continuous speculative decoding. Continuous speculative decoding leverages the diffusion model component of continuous AR models. Tokens $1\sim 3$ are prefix tokens, and token $x$ is to be verified. Upon obtaining and comparing the probability density values from the draft and target model, if $q(x)<p(x)$, $x$ is accepted. Otherwise, $x$ is rejected with probability $1-\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}$, followed by sampling from the modified distribution via acceptance-rejection sampling to obtain $x^\prime$. } \label{fig:figure_task}

elements.sent-2411-077: In LLM practice, speculative decoding utilizes a draft model $M_q$ with output distribution $q(x)$ and a target model $M_p$ with output distribution $p(x)$.

elements.subsection-2411-004: How to determine the acceptance criterion by draft and target distributions?
elements.sent-2411-088: Unlike discrete situations, $p(x)$ in continuous space represents the probability density function (PDF) of $x$.
elements.sent-2411-089: Our goal is to maintain the same distribution as PDF $p(x)$ through our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-090: Therefore, the ratio of the PDFs from draft and target output distributions can be directly obtained as a criterion of acceptance.
elements.sent-2411-091: That is, if $\frac{p(x) {q(x) >1$, the draft token is accepted.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Otherwise, we reject the token with probability $1-\frac{p(x) {q(x)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-093: The next step is to calculate $\frac{p(x) {q(x)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Since the output distribution of the continuous autoregressive model is usually obtained via diffusion process determined by DDPM~\cite{ho2020ddpm , to sample a token $x=x_0$, we sample $p(x_0|x_T)$ instead via reverse diffusion (denoising) process, where $x_T$ is a Gaussian noise.}
elements.sent-2411-095: A nice property of the denoising process is that the sampling process is a Markov chain.
elements.sent-2411-096: We can calculate $\frac{p(x) {q(x)}$ through: \begin{equation}\frac{p(x_0|x_T)}{q(x_0|x_T)}=\frac{p(x_T)\prod_{t=1}^T p(x_{t-1}|x_t)}{q(x_T)\prod_{t=1}^T q(x_{t-1}|x_t)}, \label{eqn:ratio}\end{equation} with the conditioned probability distributions $p(x_{t-1 |x_t)$ approximated as Gaussian by a neural network $\theta$~\cite{nichol202ddim : \begin{equation}p_\theta(x_{t-1}|x_t)=\mathcal{N}(x_{t-1};\mu_\theta(x_t,t),\Sigma_\theta(x_t,t)),\end{equation} where $\mu_\theta$ and $\Sigma_\theta$ are mean and variance predicted by $\theta$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: However, significant inconsistency exists in denoising processes due to the differences between draft and target models.
elements.sent-2411-098: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:figure_alignment , the denoising trajectories of the draft model and the target model diverge to different outputs, leading to distinctly different final generated distributions—the lack of consistency results in an exceedingly low acceptance probability.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{figures/alignment.pdf} \caption{ Illustration of denoising trajectory alignment. The denoising process maps the noise distribution to data distribution through gradual denoising. These denoising steps generate a trajectory. Aligned trajectories lead to similar output distribution, while unaligned one produces a more distinct distribution. } \label{fig:figure_alignment}
elements.sent-2411-099: We propose a denoising trajectory alignment strategy to enhance the consistency of the distributions generated by the denoising trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-100: The alignment ensures that both models utilize the same sampling point from the standard Gaussian distribution $\varepsilon_t\sim\mathcal{N (0,\text{I )$ at each denoising step and reparameterizes each $x_{t-1 =\sqrt{\Sigma_\theta(x_t,t)}\cdot\varepsilon_t+\mu_\theta(x_t,t)$, thereby aligning the final output distributions towards a consistent trend.}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Through the property of Gaussian distribution reparameterization, we have: \begin{equation}p(x_{t-1}|x_t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{|\Sigma_\theta(x_t,t)|}}p(\varepsilon_t).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-102: Based on this, the alignment can simplify subsequent computations.
elements.sent-2411-103: Let $\Sigma^q_t$ and $\Sigma^p_t$ be the draft and target model variance at timestep $t$.
elements.sent-2411-104: Note that $p_\theta(x^p_{t-1 |x^p_t)/q_\theta(x^q_{t-1 |x^q_t)=\sqrt{|\Sigma^q_t| /\sqrt{|\Sigma^p_t|}$ and $p(x_T)=q(x_T)$, thus we define: \begin{equation}\Sigma=\prod_{t=2}^T\sqrt{|\Sigma^q_{t}|}/\prod_{t=2}^T\sqrt{|\Sigma^p_{t}|}. \label{eqn:term}\end{equation} Substituting into Equation~\eqref{eqn:ratio yields: \begin{equation}\frac{p(x_0|x_T)}{q(x_0|x_T)}=\Sigma\cdot\frac{p_\theta(x_0|x^p_1)}{q_\theta(x_0|x^q_1)}. \label{eqn:eliminate}\end{equation} Inconsistency also exists in autoregressive steps.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: The tokens generated by the draft model during the initial autoregressive steps have a lower acceptance rate, which will be discussed in the ablation study.
elements.sent-2411-106: This phenomenon can be attributed to the different prefix embedding.
elements.sent-2411-107: MAR models of different sizes have learnable prefix embeddings of their own~\cite{li2024mar .}
elements.sent-2411-108: Naturally, these models' autoregressive predictions diverge because they have different prefix conditions.
elements.sent-2411-109: To address this issue, we propose pre-filling a portion (say $5\%$) of the tokens from the target model during autoregressive generation to obtain a relatively consistent prefix.
elements.sent-2411-110: The pre-filling does not compromise inference speed, as speculative decoding at a low acceptance rate is functionally equivalent to the step-by-step decoding performed by the target model alone~\cite{leviathan2023fast .}
elements.sent-2411-111: Furthermore, pre-filling establishes a better starting point for autoregressive generation, thereby improving the overall acceptance rate.
elements.sent-2411-112: Finally, we can calculate $p(x)/q(x)$ using the Gaussian distribution of the last denoising step and the cumulative product of variances along previous steps.
elements.subsection-2411-005: How to resample a new token from the modified distribution after $x$ is rejected?
elements.sent-2411-113: When the sample from the draft model is rejected, we resample a new token from the modified distribution $p^\prime (x)=norm(max(0,p(x)-q(x)))$ in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-114: This distribution is easy to sample because it is discrete.
elements.sent-2411-115: However, the modified distribution is hard to obtain in continuous space, and the sampling operation is difficult.
elements.sent-2411-116: Note that $p^\prime (x) =\frac{max(0,p(x)-q(x) {\sum_{x^\prime max(0,p({x^\prime )-q({x^\prime ))}$, we convert this formula in continuous-valued PDF to obtain: \begin{equation}p^\prime (x)=\frac{max(0,p(x)-q(x))}{\int_{x^\prime}max(0,p(x^\prime)-q(x^\prime))dx^\prime}.\label{eqn:modified}\end{equation} Equation~\eqref{eqn:modified represents the normalized distribution of the subtraction between the target and draft model, with only the positive part remaining.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: The functional curve of this distribution is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:figure_modified .}
elements.sent-2411-118: From this equation, we cannot compute the exact expression of this distribution because of the integration of the normalizing factor, and therefore, it cannot be directly sampled.
elements.sent-2411-119: To derive a method for sampling from Equation~\eqref{eqn:modified , we employ acceptance-rejection sampling~\cite{casella2004generalized on $p^\prime (x)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Specifically, we sample from the target model distribution $p(x)$, which is easy to sample as a proposal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-121: Then, we calculate the threshold $\alpha_s$: \begin{equation}\alpha_s=\frac{p^\prime(x)}{M\!\cdot\! p(x)}, \label{eqn:alpha}\end{equation} where $M$ is the upper bound factor that holds $M\!\cdot\!p(x)\geq p^\prime(x)$ for any $x$.
elements.sent-2411-123: We accept the sample from proposal distribution if $\epsilon<\alpha_s$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Otherwise, we reject it and resample another $x$.
elements.sent-2411-125: This sampling approach is equivalent to sampling from the modified distribution~\cite{casella2004generalized .}
elements.sent-2411-126: Note that the upper bound $M$ affects the sampling quality and should be determined properly.
elements.sent-2411-129: Therefore, we can set $M\vcentcolon=1/Z$ as the upper bound.
elements.sent-2411-130: However, the calculation of $Z$ still requires integration of $max(0,p(x)-q(x))$.
elements.sent-2411-131: Performing integration over the entire space introduces additional complex computations and potential errors.
elements.sent-2411-132: To this end, we substitute $M=1/Z$ into Equation~\eqref{eqn:alpha to obtain: \begin{equation}\alpha_s\!\!=\!\!\frac{max(0,p(x)\!\!-\!\!q(x))/Z}{p(x)/Z}\!\!=\!\!\frac{max(0,p(x)\!\!-\!\!q(x))}{p(x)}. \label{eqn:rejsample}\end{equation} Equation~\eqref{eqn:rejsample reveals that with $M=1/Z$, we can calculate $\alpha_s$ directly by the two PDFs.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Calculating the integral $Z$ can be avoided, which reduces computational complexity while maintaining the correctness of the results.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{ContinuousSpeculativeDecodingStep} \label{alg:sample_with_approx} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Inputs:} $M_p, M_q, prefix$. \STATE \COMMENTLLAMA{Sample $\gamma$ guesses $x_{1,\ldots,\gamma}$ from $M_q$ autoregressively.} \FOR{$i=1$ { to} $\gamma$} \STATE $q_i(x) \gets M_q(prefix + [x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}])$ \STATE $x_i \sim q_i(x)$ \ENDFOR \STATE \COMMENTLLAMA{Run $M_p$ in parallel, keep the $\epsilon_t$ the same in $M_q$} \STATE $p_1(x), \ldots, p_{\gamma + 1}(x) \gets$ \STATE \quad \quad $M_p(prefix), \ldots, M_p(prefix + [x_1, \ldots, x_{\gamma}])$ \STATE $\Sigma\gets\frac{\prod_{t=2}^T \sqrt{|\Sigma_{q,t}|}}{\prod_{t=2}^T\sqrt{|\Sigma_{p,t}|}}$ \STATE \COMMENTLLAMA{Determine the number of accepted guesses $n$.} \STATE $r_1 \sim U(0, 1), \dots, r_\gamma \sim U(0, 1)$ \STATE $\frac{p_i(x)}{q_i(x)}\gets\Sigma\cdot\frac{p_i(x|x^p_1)}{q_i(x|x^q_1)}$ \STATE $n \gets \min(\{ i - 1 \mid 1 \le i \le \gamma, r_i > \frac{p_i(x)}{q_i(x)} \} \cup \{ \gamma \})$ \STATE \COMMENTLLAMA{Sample the modified distribution via} \STATE \COMMENTLLAMA{ acceptance-rejection sampling.} \IF{$n < \gamma$} \REPEAT \STATE $x_t\gets p_n(x|x^p_1)$ \STATE $\alpha_s\gets\frac{max(0,\Sigma\cdot p_n(x_t|x^p_1)-q_n(x_t|x^q_1))}{\Sigma\cdot p_n(x_t|x^p_1)}$ \STATE $\epsilon\sim U(0, 1)$ \UNTIL{$\epsilon\le\alpha_s$} \ENDIF \STATE \COMMENTLLAMA{Return one token from $M_p$, and $n$ tokens from $M_q$.} \STATE { return} $prefix + [x_1, \ldots, x_{n}, x_t]$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-288: Continuous visual autoregressive (AR) models have demonstrated promising performance in image generation.
elements.sent-2411-289: However, the heavy autoregressive inference burden imposes significant overhead.
elements.sent-2411-290: In Large Language Models (LLMs), speculative decoding has effectively accelerated discrete autoregressive inference.
elements.sent-2411-291: However, the absence of an analogous theory for continuous distributions precludes its use in accelerating continuous AR models.
elements.sent-2411-292: To fill this gap, this work presents continuous speculative decoding, and addresses challenges from: 1) low acceptance rate, caused by inconsistent output distribution between target and draft models, and 2) modified distribution without analytic expression, caused by complex integral.
elements.sent-2411-293: To address challenge 1), we propose denoising trajectory alignment and token pre-filling strategies.
elements.sent-2411-294: To address challenge 2), we introduce acceptance-rejection sampling algorithm with an appropriate upper bound, thereby avoiding explicitly calculating the integral.
elements.sent-2411-295: Furthermore, our denoising trajectory alignment is also reused in acceptance-rejection sampling, effectively avoiding repetitive diffusion model inference.
elements.sent-2411-296: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed continuous speculative decoding achieves over $2\times$ speedup on off-the-shelf models, while maintaining the original generation quality.
elements.sent-2411-298: Autoregressive (AR) models have demonstrated significant potential and achieved competitive performance in image generation tasks~\citep{van2016pixelrnn,van2016pixelcnn,esser2021taming,yu2023magvit, VAR .}
elements.sent-2411-299: These models predict next token sequentially based on previously generated tokens, outputting discrete categorical distributions.
elements.sent-2411-300: Typically, the input image is mapped from the pixel space to tokens through vector quantization (VQ), after which the AR model generates images by predicting discrete distributions for subsequent tokens.
elements.sent-2411-301: While this process shows substantial promise in image generation, VQ can lead to training instability and might not adequately capture the nuanced image details~\citep{yu2023magvit,mentzer2023finite .}
elements.sent-2411-302: Recently, continuous visual AR models have been proposed to predict visual tokens from continuous probability distributions~\citep{tschannen2025givt,li2024mar,xu2024discodiff,ren2025xar .}
elements.sent-2411-303: In this framework, the model predicts next token's continuous distribution based on prior outputs, often implemented via denoising processes~\citep{ho2020ddpm,nichol202ddim .}
elements.sent-2411-304: This approach overcomes the limitations of VQ, offering a promising solution for autoregressive image generation.
elements.figure-2411-027: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{figures/intro.pdf} \caption{ Comparison between discrete and continuous speculative decoding. Discrete situation offers the convenience of directly computing probabilities and simply sampling from modified distributions. In contrast, continuous situation faces challenges in the inconsistency of output distributions, leading to low acceptance criterion as well as low acceptance rate, and the modified distributions without analytic expression, caused by complex integral. } \label{fig:figure_intro}
elements.sent-2411-305: However, continuous visual AR models suffer from slow inference speed due to the sequential decoding process, a limitation shared with LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-306: LLMs commonly employ speculative decoding~\citep{leviathan2023fast,chen2023acc to accelerate autoregressive inference procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-307: This algorithm employs a draft-and-verification mechanism, where a smaller draft model generates several draft tokens, and then a more accurate yet often larger target model verifies them.
elements.sent-2411-308: Inspired by this, previous works have investigated the efficacy of speculative decoding for accelerating discrete visual AR models~\citep{jang2024lantern,teng2024sjd .}
elements.sent-2411-309: However, the related methods are formulated for discrete output distributions, whereas a continuous formulation remains undeveloped.
elements.sent-2411-310: To bridge this gap, we present Continuous Speculative Decoding, designed to accelerate the inference of continuous visual AR models.
elements.sent-2411-311: However, as illustrated in \Figref{fig:figure_intro , the following two challenges can be identified while developing this method: a) Inconsistent output distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-312: There is significant inconsistency in diffusion and autoregressive procedures, resulting in distinct draft and target output distributions.
elements.sent-2411-313: This leads to a very low acceptance criterion, thus lowering the acceptance rate.
elements.sent-2411-314: When an output token is rejected, a new token will be drawn from the modified distribution.
elements.sent-2411-315: In continuous distributions, there is no analytic expression for this due to the complex integral for normalizing the probability distribution, making it impossible to directly sample from this distribution.
elements.sent-2411-316: To address the aforementioned challenges, this work proposes a series of technical optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-317: First, to improve the acceptance rate, we develop denoising trajectory alignment method based on the proposed theorem of proximity in the reparameterization.
elements.sent-2411-318: This method aims to reduce the distance between two output distributions, thereby resolving the inconsistency in the diffusion procedure.
elements.sent-2411-319: Additionally, we introduce a token pre-filling strategy to improve the early low acceptance rate.
elements.sent-2411-320: This strategy leverages the principle that the output of AR models depends on previous states, addressing inconsistencies within the autoregressive procedure.
elements.sent-2411-321: Second, to effectively sample from the complex modified distribution, we introduce the acceptance-rejection sampling algorithm~\citep{casella2004generalized by deriving a proper upper bound, which helps to eliminate the need for computing complex integral.}
elements.sent-2411-322: Besides, to avoid repetitive use of diffusion model for inference during acceptance-rejection sampling, we derive an easy-to-compute rejection threshold by reusing the denoising trajectory alignment.
elements.sent-2411-323: Similar to traditional speculative decoding, continuous speculative decoding can also be proven to maintain the original distribution of target model.
elements.sent-2411-324: Our continuous speculative decoding can be integrated seamlessly into many of existing models, as shown in \Figref{fig:teaser .}
elements.sent-2411-325: We validate the effectiveness of our algorithm on three continuous visual AR models~\citep{li2024mar,ren2025xar,wu2025harmon at two resolutions (256 \& 512) through qualitative and quantitative evaluations.}
elements.sent-2411-326: Specifically, we measure wall-time improvements and report image generation performance using Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)~\citep{heusel2017fid and Inception Score (IS)~\citep{salimans2016is .}}
elements.sent-2411-327: Extensive experiments show that our algorithm achieves over $2\times$ inference speedup while maintaining generation quality.
elements.sent-2411-328: We are the first to propose continuous speculative decoding, bridging the gap of speculative decoding to continuous distributions and enabling substantial acceleration of continuous visual AR models.
elements.sent-2411-329: We address the distinct problem of low acceptance rate in continuous speculative decoding by introducing two novel techniques: denoising trajectory alignment, which reduces the draft-target distribution distance, and token pre-filling, which mitigates the early low acceptance rate problem.
elements.sent-2411-330: We solve the hard-to-sample problem of modified distribution via acceptance-rejection sampling with a proper upper bound to avoid the complex integral and an easy-to-compute rejection threshold with denoising trajectory alignment to avoid repetitive model inference.
elements.sent-2411-331: We seamlessly integrate our algorithm into three existing continuous visual AR models without extra training or architectural changes.
elements.sent-2411-332: Extensive experiments show that it achieves over $2\times$ inference speedup while maintaining generation quality.

elements.figure-2411-028: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{figures/main.pdf} \caption{ The overview of continuous speculative decoding. The diffusion model component of continuous AR models is leveraged. Tokens $1\sim 3$ are prefix tokens, and token $x$ is to be verified. In this method, $x$ is accepted if $q(x)<p(x)$. Otherwise, $x$ is rejected with probability $1-{p(x)}/{q(x)}$, followed by sampling $x^\prime$ from the modified distribution via acceptance-rejection sampling. } \label{fig:figure_task}
elements.subsection-2411-017: From Discrete to Continuous Speculative Decoding
elements.sent-2411-352: We first introduce the discrete form of speculative decoding.
elements.sent-2411-353: It utilizes a draft model $M_q$ with output distribution $q(x)$ and a target model $M_p$ with $p(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-354: Then, we discuss continuous speculative decoding (see \Figref{fig:figure_task ).}
elements.sent-2411-355: In continuous visual AR models~\citep{li2024mar , the output distribution is typically modeled via diffusion models, namely: \begin{equation}p(x_N) =p(x_{N,0}|x_{N,T},x_{1:N-1}) =p(x_{N,T})\prod_{t=1}^Tp(x_{N,t-1}|x_{N,t},x_{1:N-1}), \label{eqn:first_define}\end{equation} where $x_N$ represents the variable at the $N$-th autoregressive step.}
elements.sent-2411-356: For simplicity, we omit $N$ and $x_{1:N-1}$ to obtain $p(x)=p(x_0|x_T)$.
elements.sent-2411-357: For a token $x$, the acceptance criterion is defined as the ratio of its probability under target distribution to the one under draft distribution, that is, $p(x)/q(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-358: In discrete form, the probability can be directly obtained.
elements.sent-2411-359: But in continuous form, the probability is usually obtained via diffusion process~\citep{ho2020ddpm,song2020denoisingddim .}
elements.sent-2411-360: Specifically, to sample $x=x_0$, we sample $p(x_0|x_T)$ via reverse diffusion (denoising) process, where $x_T$ is a Gaussian noise.
elements.sent-2411-361: Then, ${p(x) /{q(x)}$ is calculated through: \begin{equation}\frac{p(x_0|x_T)}{q(x_0|x_T)}=\frac{p(x_T)\prod_{t=1}^T p(x_{t-1}|x_t)}{q(x_T)\prod_{t=1}^T q(x_{t-1}|x_t)}, \label{eqn:ratio}\end{equation} where $p(x_{t-1 |x_t)$ is approximated as a Gaussian distribution through a network $\theta$~\citep{nichol202ddim , and $\mu_\theta(x_t,t)$ and $\Sigma_\theta(x_t,t)$ are mean and variance predicted by $\theta$: \begin{equation}p(x_{t-1}|x_t)=\mathcal{N}(x_{t-1};\mu_\theta(x_t,t),\Sigma_\theta(x_t,t)). \label{eqn:normal}\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-362: When a draft token is rejected, a new one is sampled from the modified distribution $p^\prime (x)$.
elements.sent-2411-363: Therefore, replacing the summation in the normalization denominator of the discrete form with integral yields the continuous form, namely: \begin{equation}p^\prime (x)=\frac{max(0,p(x)-q(x))}{Z},\,\text{where}\, Z=\int_{x^\prime}max(0,p(x^\prime)-q(x^\prime))dx^\prime.\label{eqn:modified}\end{equation} Theoretically, the final output distribution can also be shown to preserve the original distribution under continuous settings, as we demonstrate in Appendix~\ref{app:correct .}
elements.sent-2411-364: However, although theoretical correctness can be guaranteed, in practical operation, an extremely low acceptance rate emerges, as shown in Table~\ref{tab:acceptance , thereby leading to poor acceleration performance.}
elements.sent-2411-365: To address this, we propose the following technical optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-366: Under the acceptance criterion given by \Eqref{eqn:ratio , the acceptance rate $\alpha$ is extremely small (nearly 0\%).}
elements.sent-2411-367: We attribute this to two sources of inconsistency in continuous visual AR models: 1) inconsistency in diffusion process, and 2) inconsistency in autoregressive process.
elements.sent-2411-368: Significant inconsistency exists in the denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-369: As illustrated in \Figref{fig:figure_alignment , draft and target denoising trajectories diverge to different outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-370: The distance between the two distributions is large.
elements.sent-2411-371: Therefore, for a draft token $x$, $q(x)$ is large while $p(x)$ is quite small, leading to a low ${p(x) /{q(x)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-372: Low ${p(x) /{q(x)}$ leads to quite low acceptance rate $\alpha$.}

The denoising process maps the noise distribution to data distribution through gradual denoising. These denoising steps form a trajectory. The aligned trajectory (orange curve) leads to a similar output distribution, while the unaligned one (red curve) produces a far-away one, obtaining low ${p(x)}/{q(x)}$. } \label{fig:figure_alignment} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.36\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/modified_distribution.pdf} \caption{ Illustration of distributions. Dashed lines: draft and target distributions. Red area: modified distribution (unnormalized, omitting $Z$ for simplicity), whose integral lack an analytic expression. } \label{fig:figure_modified} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-373: We propose denoising trajectory alignment to enhance the consistency of the output distributions.
elements.sent-2411-374: Note that in \Eqref{eqn:normal , $x_{t-1 ^p$ is obtained via reparameterization given by $x_{t-1 ^p=\sqrt{\Sigma^p_\theta(x^p_t,t)}\cdot\varepsilon_t^p+\mu^p_\theta(x^p_t,t)$, where $\varepsilon_t^p\sim\mathcal{N (0,\text{I )$ (same for $x_{t-1 ^q$).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-375: Denoising trajectory alignment can reduce the expected distance between $x^p_{t-1}$ and $x^q_{t-1}$, promised by the following theorem.

elements.sent-2411-378: For simplicity, we define: \begin{equation}\Sigma=\prod_{t=2}^T\sqrt{|\Sigma^q_{t}|}/\prod_{t=2}^T\sqrt{|\Sigma^p_{t}|}. \label{eqn:term}\end{equation} Substituting $\Sigma$ into \Eqref{eqn:ratio makes (assuming $p(x_T)=q(x_T)$): \begin{equation}\frac{p(x_0|x_T)}{q(x_0|x_T)} =\frac{p(x_T)p(x_0|x_1^p)\prod_{t=2}^Tp(x^p_{t-1}|x^p_t)}{q(x_T)q(x_0|x_1^q)\prod_{t=2}^Tq(x^q_{t-1}|x^q_t)} =\frac{p_\theta(x_0|x^p_1)}{q_\theta(x_0|x^q_1)}\cdot \Sigma. \label{eqn:eliminate}\end{equation} Therefore, at each step, employing the same $\varepsilon_t$ reduces the expected distance by $2\cdot\text{tr}\left[\Sigma^q_t\Sigma^p_t\right]$, which enables the two models to generate closer samples and finally increases the acceptance rate.}
